text: Patient is in the supine position with the operating surgeon to the right of the patient.
In this video, the cranial end of the patient is towards the left.
Following a standard median sternotomy, the thymus is resected off.
after clipping the thymic vein of canes.
The portion of the pericardium adherent to the mass is excised.
Control of the left brachiocephalic vein is obtained both distally as well as proximally in relation to the mass.
The brachiocephalic vein is dissected of the underlying tumor.
Dissection of the tumor itself commences with an attempt to free it from all the neighboring structures.
Control traction on the brachiocephalic vein along with the use of a vein loop aids in the dissection of this tumor.
The tumor feeding vessels are ligated and divided.
The left pleura is then opened revealing the left lung.
The left vagus nerve is seen here medially and the left phrenic nerve infiltrated and pushed laterally by the mass.
The left phrenic nerve is clipped and divided.
Spatial relations of the left vagus nerve are shown here for perspective.
We proceed further by meticulously dissecting
the left vagus nerve along its entire course, preserving the recurrent laryngeal nerve as it hooks around the arch of aorta.
Pericardium adherent to the mass is excised.
In this case, we used a vicryl mesh for reconstructing the pericardium.
The vagus is traced further distally, successfully preserving the nerve in its entire course.
Left hilar structures are delineated by meticulous dissection.
And the left main pulmonary artery is looped.
The left main pulmonary artery is dissected, freeing it by taking any small vessels that supply the tumor.
A direct branch from the left main pulmonary artery was seen entering the mass, which was
clipped and divided.
This is a panoramic view showing the entire left hemithorax occupied by the tumor after its dissection from the mediastinal structures.
The tumor is further freed from the remnant lung with wedge resections.
In this particular case, we were able to get the entire tumor with small wedge resections of the upper and the lower lobes, sparing most of the lung parenchyma.
However, in the interest of oncologic clearance, we would not hesitate to proceed with a pneumonectomy in cases which do not permit a conservation.
With the caveat of permissible pulmonary functions.
The specimen is delivered.
with the remnant lung seen to be expanding well.
We proceed with the supraclavicular nodal clearance.
